segments.text: So we welcome you commissioner
to the select committee this afternoon.
Uh, we all have, uh, this report now, just, uh, members,
the primary production committee was going to be joining us,
but, uh, they aren't
because they've run over on their session.
So I understand you may do a private briefing
with them, a separate one.
So, uh, look, Simon, we will hand it over
to you for our remarks.
Actually, just before we start,
because Barbara has just walked into the room, um, uh,
just gonna seek leave of the committee for Barbara PR
to participate, um, in this briefing this afternoon.
Yes. Hearing Okay. We have Deborah come in. Yes.
And we may have, and other members join as long
as members are, are happy to, to grant me for
that participation.
Thank you. Okay. Well, we will hand it over to you
for some opening remarks,
and yes, be mindful that there'll be plenty
of questions, I'm sure.
Sure. Thank you, Madam Chair.
Uh, I have with me Dr. Anna Hooper and, uh, Dr. Uh, Nora.
Ari. These are the two people who've done a lot
of the spade work here
and Matt Patterson, whom I think, you know, uh, I'd like
to present the key findings from my latest report,
which is entitled, reviewing the use
of the LUC System in a regulatory context,
which was published last December.
Let me start by outlining
what the LUC system is.
LUC stands for Land Use Capability.
It's a land classification system,
which was developed in the 1950s based on, uh,
an American approach.
It was designed to support long-term sustained
production on the land.
So we did this because we wanted
to know about the productive capability of, uh, the land.
Um, it, there's eight classes.
I think most of you probably know this, LUC one to eight.
Uh, within each of those classes, uh, there are subclasses.
The subclasses are, uh, identified on the basis
of the dominant limitation for production.
There's four potential limitations.
I mean, all of them might apply,
but only the, the predominant one is then subclassified.
They are erod ability, wetness, soil
and climate, um, and that then there are units within those.
But this is the key point.
It was never designed to address environmental challenges.
It was to tell us what, what our land could produce.
So the first question you might ask is, why review the use
of the NUC system for a biological economy like ours?
High quality information about the land
and the wider landscape is critical,
and it is also critical for high quality, uh,
environmental regulation.
Currently, the LUC system is being relied on
to support three pieces of national regulation.
These are the national policy statement
for highly productive land,
the national environmental standard for commercial forestry
and regulation under the Climate Change Response Act
to limit ETS driven fund forestry conversions.
In addition, the LUC system is being used in regional
regulatory plans in the context of water quality
and nutrient management.
Now, given that it wasn't designed
to address environmental management challenges,
but it's now being used to support regulations,
it's prudent, I think,
to review whether the system is sufficiently robust,
particularly if the proposed new planning system is going
to have much more national direction.
The key finding of the review is
that the LUC system is ill suited for regulatory use,
especially for use of the property scale.
And that's due to several limitations.
The first is that the LUC system is outdated.
This is because the main mapping which underlies it,
was carried out in the 1970s and 1980s.
So it's around half a century ago.
And that mapping remains unchanged.
So it doesn't reflect the situation today
because land management practices such as irrigation
or drainage have directly affected the subclass limitation
of, say, wetness, for instance.
So think about Canterbury, which has been transformed, uh,
by irrigation now at the time
of the mapping, when, and,
and they did an amazing job with what they had.
So this is, we're not
being critical of the people who did it.
They did a very good job at the time,
but they had to use, uh, manual technologies
and a high degree of expert judgment.
Um, and that unavoidably introduces a high degree
of subjectivity into the areas or the polygons on the maps
and the boundaries between them.
The second, uh, limitation is that the core scale
of the mapping makes the national
LUC data set in appropriate for use at a property scale.
So the scale, uh,
of the national LUC data set is one to 50,000.
So that means a centimeter on the map equals 500
meters on the ground.
But to make operational
or site-based decisions, you need maps at the scale of say,
one to 5,000, one to 10,000 or whatever.
A centimeter equals 50 meters or a hundred meters.
Now, as any landowner knows,
and that there must be a couple in the room, I'm one,
the limitations of land can change much more frequently than
at 500 meter intervals.
I know from my own farm
that paddocks can move from well drained to not
so well drained much, much more swiftly than, uh,
50 uh, meters.
In fact, I'm having some work done
by a student at present just
to understand some of this variability.
It's really interesting stuff.
Now, let me outline how these limitations
of the LUC system play out in existing national regulations.
I'll start with highly productive land.
Let's look at a tangible example from the Hawke's Bay
where the council explored very familiar.
No, it wasn't chosen because of you, Madame Chair.
It just, it
just happens that they've got very good
information in Hawke's Bay.
Yes. But, um, this, this is what the, the regional council,
uh, explored to identify highly productive land.
Now, if you look at this map,
so this is just south of Hastings, okay?
Mm-hmm. The pink colors on the map show areas
of LUC classes, one to three identified using the national
LUC dataset.
And if you look at the second map on the right
with the yellow, it shows the class one to three land
that shows up when you use an alternative dataset.
In this case, the digital soil map s map,
which is much more recent.
And as you may be able to see, uh, at a glimpse, uh,
the areas don't match.
There's a bigger version for you.
So the teal color areas show the overlap between the two.
So the old one, the old stuff is the pink
and the newer SAP is the yellow,
and they only overlap over 41% of the land area.
Mm-hmm. The rest doesn't, the significant disagreement
between the two data sets, similar analysis in Canterbury,
by the way, showed an even lower level of agreement
around 21%.
The point is that it would need substantial ground truthing
to reconcile and validate the two.
You'd be ill-advised to rely solely on one
or either of the methods for identification
of Hadi productive land and practical terms.
If you were concerned,
and you might be that being classed
as highly productive could limit your land use options, then
that lack of overlap could spill real problems.
Just look at the pink bits south, just south of Hastings.
Hastings is the top of the map there,
and there's teal stuff.
So they both agree, but then there's some quite big bits
of pink below that.
Mm. Now the digital soil map doesn't give these a high
status, but LUC does.
So you could find yourself in an argument
with a regulator if you were in those places.
The second illustration I've chosen of
how the LEC system plays out is the national environmental
standard on commercial forestry.
Now, the N-E-S-C-F uses the erosion susceptibility
classification, that's called ESC for short, um,
which has been derived from the LUC system.
And it's a screening tool
or threshold test to identify the erosion
susceptibility of land intended for forestry.
The threshold test is used
to determine whether a forestry activity is permitted
subject to certain conditions being met,
or whether No, no, you've gotta get a resource consent.
So it it, it's, it's what's used
to triage looking at the land's erosion susceptibility.
Now, given that erosion from forestry activities is a key
environmental issue, as we know from RAF
and elsewhere, that the,
this environmental standard is supposed to manage.
Identifying the risky land accurately is really critical.
That relies on the robustness of the erosion classification
and the national ESC data set.
So let's just look at practice at what this means.
Unfortunately, the ESC erosion susceptibility
both over and under classifies susceptibility
of land to erosion.
Now, under classifying land is a problem environmentally
because it gives the green light
to forestry when maybe more questions should be asked in
stricter controls imposed.
Let's explore again with some analysis from the Hawke's Bay.
This map shows three, uh, ESC zones,
lovely smooth edges, green with low erosion susceptibility,
orange with high susceptibility
and red with very high susceptibility.
There was a fourth yellow zone for medium susceptibility,
but there wasn't any on this map.
Okay, so there's a, there's a, there's a color missing,
but for this map, there's only three,
three susceptibilities.
Just note the uniformity and ness of these zones.
That reflects the fact that this all goes back to a one
to 50,000 data set.
Okay, now look at the green zone on the second map.
So it's not, it's the identical area.
Just look at that green zone, but go down to the second map.
This adds in recent research from what was
to recently called landcare research, me federa,
which reveals that this area contains areas
of high susceptibility to landslides
and high connectivity to waterways.
And that's the red. So you can see there's plenty
of red bits in the green zone.
So here, the ESC under classifies the land.
Now, as I've mentioned, under classifying,
it lands a real risk because of the more permissive approach
the environmental standard takes to management of that land.
Now, there are of course, gonna be instances
where ESC over classifies the land
and says, we should be more cautious than we should be.
But, but the, the fact of the matter is you can't rely on
that ESC alone.
And here, just by way of interest, this third map,
we have added some additional information.
Overlaying research from Earth Sciences New Zealand,
the old GNS, this shows areas in purple
with a greater than 80% probability
of rainfall induced landslides.
Again, it shows just
how coarse the ESC zones are in comparison
with the recent research output.
But I was keen to include this slide
because I think it shows something of the power of newer,
finer resolution data sets.
The problem is
that we do not have them over all of New Zealand.
There's lots and lots and lots of gaps.
And even when we've got them,
they haven't been gathered necessarily in a way that's easy
to stitch them together for the purpose
that you're, you're concerned about.
So that brings me to my recommendations.
Uh, the first is that as a result
of the limitations I've outlined,
the current LUC system should not be used
as a basis for any future environmental regulation.
This recommendation applies both to national
and regional regulation through RMA plans.
The use of the national LUC data set
for property scale regulation used regularly in RMA plans is
particularly problematic.
Secondly, the government should invest in progressively
and proving essential contemporary data sets
that underpin land use regulation
and ensure they are publicly available and affordable
and can be easily integrated.
It's one thing to have them,
but then if you wanna put them together, you need to be sure
that they gathered in a way which enables that to happen.
As part of the review, uh,
we've identified several such data sets.
Lidars one, very accurate information on
elevation as well as slope.
And that's used for a gazillion applications hazard
management, forestry engineering, flood management
S map, which I've mentioned.
Digital soil map provides associated information
about soil properties.
There's something called the land cover database,
which tells us what's happening with land cover, you know,
forest versus grassland versus wetland versus urban versus
bare soil versus water bodies.
Uh, that one, um,
I think it's undertaken every set or eight years.
And then we need,
obviously comprehensive real time land use mapping
to support spatial planning decisions at
the national and local level.
Such maps don't currently exist.
I mean, there's lots and lots of land use maps,
but we do not have a common methodology.
Mm-hmm. There are satellites flying over
New Zealand all the time.
We do not have that information.
I just thought you'd like to see what LIDAR tells
you about Parliament.
Thi I think we are in a
building, a building.
Thirdly, these essential contemporary data sets,
and I've only mentioned a handful.
There's, there's many, many more than that. A handful.
They need to be used to modernize that LUC and ESC systems.
You don't throw the baby up with the bath water, but,
but they need to be modernized.
But I think more importantly, we are suitable.
We need to create bespoke fit for purpose models
for specific regulatory purposes.
And I think you can imagine a world in which national
direction could specify the data requirements
and layers needed to address particular problems.
But you have to have the layers
and you have to have the data, and it has to be in a form.
You can use it to do that.
Finally, we need to focus improvements on areas
where better information is most urgently needed.
I know the stuff is costly
and there are limited resources, so you need
to be able to prioritize.
And that is what a government should do, for example,
in the context of the, uh,
highly productive land regulation.
You need to focus getting the data right in those areas,
which are under pressure from urban expansion.
Mm-hmm. So that's Auckland, Tara Hamilton,
Napier Hastings, Christchurch.
I mean, it's obvious, isn't it?
Finally, some concluding remarks.
Uh, this review has highlighted
that the LUC system isn't fit for purpose
for certainly from environmental regulation.
Uh, and the sorts of uses it's been put to most recently
for the farm forestry conversion, uh, thing.
And that's really what got me, uh, going.
The limitations of that system are yet another example
of the broader issue, which I keep drawing
to this committee's attention.
And that is the state of our environmental information.
The need for better information, which is the foundation
for good decision making, uh, has been a consistent theme
that I have raised with you now
and some of your predecessors for eight years.
The new resource management system proposes
to make greater use of spatial planning
and environmental limits.
These will require much, much better information
than we have currently available.
If you're gonna have limits which can stand up in court,
which you can justify to your taxpayers rate, payers
and citizens, they need
to be based on good quality information that is not there.
In many cases. I can't stress to you enough
how critical good environmental data
and information is for environmental management.
I would go further and suggest
that without significant investment in the underlying
environmental data and information I proposed,
planning and environmental management system will fail.
With that, we'd be very happy to answer questions,
particularly my colleagues on any of the detail around, uh,
the science behind this. Hmm.
Look, thank you very much, Simon.
That really insightful, really interesting.
And look, I might just take the opportunity
to ask the first question, given
that you have given Hawks Bay as an example,
and given that, uh, you know, it is, it is an issue, uh,
you know, the luck one, two, and three, um,
and all on highly productive land Hastings, uh,
Tonga Plains, um, and you know, in terms of development,
and you just said, uh, you know, it is expensive to look at,

you know, government, if you were

Maybe not Hawkes Bay, because maybe we,
that we may actually have better information there.

Uh, yeah. Uh, and obviously focusing on the soil,

But obviously there are plenty of gaps specifically for the,

So it's not, uh, providing maybe as much help

Well, we didn't specifically look into this as part

but we're, uh, well aware,
especially if you look at Australia,
there are quite a few data sets
and the, uh, obviously, you know, huge country,
but equally more resources

especially from the research community.
Hmm. Uh, I, uh, so it's maybe the question of ensuring

but also to support, uh, management

Just on that, can, can I tell a committee,
I'll have a report with you around the middle of the year,

which we'll pull together a lot of
what I've been saying about environmental information.
I've already, um, sent ministers in advance of coming

But we produced a piece about integrated,
uh, environmental data.
Um, I'm going to pull that together with an assessment of,
um, the, the way in which some of the new technologies, uh,

Mm-hmm. Now, is New Zealand ahead or behind?
I mean, look, we've got very good scientists in pockets

We've got a slew of national data sets.

you know, a, a penny here,
a penny there as they could put it together.

Um, so this is, this is the moment for this committee

and you probably segued into where I was going.

'cause uh, regions, uh, often, uh, have occasions

we don't have the information.

Sure. You know, not far the ordinary farming operation,

Thursday, 12th February 2026 | Environment Committee

Report of the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment, Reviewing the use of the Land Use Capability system in a regulatory context

Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
Simon Upton, Commissioner
